article_title: I switched to these 5 Linux apps on Windows and cancelled my subscriptions
core_thesis: The article argues that many popular open-source Linux applications are fully compatible with Windows and can replace expensive subscription-based software like Microsoft 365, Adobe Creative Cloud, 1Password, and Notion, allowing users to save money without sacrificing functionality.
detailed_summary: The author, Anurag Singh, shares his experience of replacing paid software with five open-source alternatives that run on Windows. He highlights that these apps are often associated with Linux but have official Windows versions. The five apps are: LibreOffice (replaces Microsoft 365), Kdenlive (replaces Adobe Premiere Pro), GIMP (replaces Adobe Photoshop), Bitwarden (replaces 1Password), and Joplin (replaces Notion). The article emphasizes that these tools cover essential productivity, video editing, image editing, password management, and note-taking needs. The author also notes that some of these apps can be self-hosted for greater control. The article concludes by advocating for open-source software as a viable, cost-effective alternative to subscription services.

entities.name: Anurag Singh
entities.role: Author of the article, tech journalist

entities.name: Adobe
entities.role: Provider of paid creative software (Photoshop, Premiere Pro)

entities.name: Notion
entities.role: Paid note-taking and project management app

key_data_points: LibreOffice is a free, open-source office suite that includes word processor, spreadsheet, presentation, drawing, and database tools, and can open/edit DOCX, XLSX, PPTX files, making it a viable replacement for Microsoft 365 (🟢 relevant to your student needs: saves money on subscription fees for academic work).

Kdenlive is a non-linear video editor supporting multi-track editing, transitions, audio mixing, and a wide range of formats, serving as a free alternative to Adobe Premiere Pro (🟢 relevant to your Red Teaming: can be used to edit video PoCs or tutorials without subscription).

GIMP is an open-source image editor with layers, masks, selection tools, and plugins, suitable for photo editing and graphic design, replacing Adobe Photoshop (🟢 relevant to your programming: useful for creating UI mockups or thumbnails for projects).

Bitwarden is a password manager that stores login details, secure notes, and payment info in an encrypted vault, with self-hosting options, replacing 1Password (🟢 relevant to your cybersecurity: essential for managing credentials securely in homelab and red team operations).

Joplin is a Markdown-based note-taking app that supports attachments, code blocks, and self-hosting, replacing Notion (🟢 relevant to your student needs: great for organizing research, code snippets, and study notes with full control).

All five apps are free and open-source, with official Windows versions, contradicting the misconception that they are Linux-only (🟢 relevant to your homelab/devops: demonstrates cross-platform compatibility for self-hosted services).
conclusions: Open-source applications like LibreOffice, Kdenlive, GIMP, Bitwarden, and Joplin can effectively replace paid subscriptions for office suites, video editing, image editing, password management, and note-taking, saving users significant money. The article provides evidence through the author's personal switch and highlights that these tools are mature and feature-rich.

The article underscores the value of self-hosting for privacy and control, particularly with Bitwarden and Joplin, which aligns with the user's interest in homelabbing and cybersecurity. By self-hosting, users can secure their data and reduce reliance on third-party cloud services.

Despite some commenters noting that these apps have long been available on Windows, the article's core message remains valid: users can cancel expensive subscriptions by adopting open-source alternatives. The author's experience shows that the transition is practical for everyday tasks.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to you as a programmer, cybersecurity enthusiast, and student. It provides a list of free, open-source tools that can replace costly subscriptions, saving money while offering robust features. For your red teaming work, Bitwarden is crucial for secure credential management, and Kdenlive/GIMP can be used to create professional-looking PoCs or documentation. As a student, LibreOffice and Joplin are excellent for academic work and note-taking. The article also encourages self-hosting, which aligns with your homelab interests. You can immediately adopt these tools to reduce expenses and gain more control over your software stack.
extra_advice: Consider self-hosting Bitwarden and Joplin on your homelab to enhance security and privacy. For red teaming, explore Bitwarden's CLI and API for automated credential management in scripts. Additionally, try integrating Joplin with Git for version-controlled notes, which is useful for tracking changes in your study materials or project documentation.
